{"content": "import type { Sop } from '@/types'

// Just-in-time SOP / knowledge cards. The Copilot "Ask" skill keyword-matches
// against these to return grounded answers during the demo.
export const SOPS: Sop[] = [
  {
    id: 'sop-endcap',
    title: 'Build & verify a promotional end-cap',
    domainId: 'merchandising',
    department: 'Gaming',
    summary: 'How to set up a promotional end-cap so it is compliant and selling within 30 minutes.',
    keywords: ['endcap', 'end cap', 'display', 'promo', 'merchandising', 'gondola', 'set up display', 'console bundle'],
    steps: [
      'Collect the promo kit (header card, shelf tickets, barker cards) from the comms folder.',
      'Clear the gondola end and clean the shelves before building.',
      'Place hero stock at eye level; ensure at least 6 facings of the bundle.',
      'Apply the bundle ticket and check the price matches the promo guidance.',
      'Power the demo unit and load the featured title/loop.',
      'Photograph the finished end-cap and mark the task complete to verify compliance.',
    ],
    sourceId: 'src-quorso',
  },
  {
    id: 'sop-age-restricted',
    title: 'Age-restricted sale & refusal process (Challenge 25)',
    domainId: 'loss-prevention',
    summary: 'Selling PEGI 18 games and other age-restricted lines, and how to log a refusal.',
    keywords: ['age', 'age restricted', 'pegi', 'pegi 18', 'challenge 25', 'refusal', 'id check', 'underage'],
    steps: [
      'If the customer looks under 25, ask for valid photo ID (passport, driving licence, PASS card).',
      'Check the date of birth confirms they are 18 or over for PEGI 18 titles.',
      'If ID is not provided or is invalid, politely refuse the sale.',
      'Log the refusal in the refusals register with time and reason.',
      'Never feel pressured — escalate aggression to the duty manager / security.',
    ],
    sourceId: 'src-requirements',
  },
  {
    id: 'sop-trade-in',
    title: 'Process a smartphone trade-in + cashback',
    domainId: 'returns-service',
    department: 'Mobile & Wearables',
    summary: 'Taking an old handset in trade and registering the cashback claim.',
    keywords: ['trade-in', 'trade in', 'cashback', 'phone', 'handset', 'part exchange'],
    steps: [
      'Run the device through the trade-in calculator on the kiosk to grade it.',
      'Confirm the customer has signed out of all accounts and removed the SIM/SD card.',
      'Factory-reset the device and verify it powers to setup screen.',
      'Apply the trade-in value against the new handset at the till.',
      'Help the customer scan the cashback QR card to register their claim.',
    ],
    sourceId: 'src-currys',
  },
  {
    id: 'sop-click-collect',
    title: 'Click & Collect handover and ID verification',
    domainId: 'fulfilment',
    department: 'Customer Service',
    summary: 'Handing over a collection order correctly, including ID checks for high-value items.',
    keywords: ['click and collect', 'click & collect', 'collection', 'handover', 'fast track', 'pick up', 'id verification'],
    steps: [
      'Ask for the order reference and the collector’s name.',
      'For high-value or age-restricted items, verify photo ID matches the order.',
      'Retrieve the order from the staging area and check the items against the pick list.',
      'Confirm the customer is happy, then scan to close the collection.',
      'Capture proof of collection (signature or scan) on the handheld.',
    ],
    sourceId: 'src-argos',
  },
  {
    id: 'sop-temperature-log',
    title: 'Stockroom temperature & equipment check',
    domainId: 'safety-compliance',
    summary: 'Daily temperature log for the stockroom chiller and the staff vending area.',
    keywords: ['temperature', 'temp log', 'fridge', 'chiller', 'compliance check', 'safety check'],
    steps: [
      'Read the chiller thermometer and record the temperature on the digital form.',
      'Acceptable range is 1°C to 5°C — if outside, raise an equipment fault immediately.',
      'Check the door seal and that nothing is blocking airflow.',
      'Sign and timestamp the log; the task captures this as evidence.',
    ],
    sourceId: 'src-axonify',
  },
  {
    id: 'sop-equipment-fault',
    title: 'Log an equipment / facilities fault',
    domainId: 'equipment-it',
    summary: 'Reporting a broken till, display fridge, printer or facilities issue to the right team.',
    keywords: ['fault', 'broken', 'repair', 'facilities', 'till down', 'printer', 'fridge', 'equipment', 'escalate'],
    steps: [
      'Make the area safe and put any affected equipment out of use.',
      'Open a fault ticket and select the category (IT for tills/printers, Facilities for fridges/heating/lighting).',
      'Add a short description and a photo of the issue.',
      'The system routes it to the right team with an SLA — track it on the Escalations board.',
    ],
    sourceId: 'src-axonify',
  },
  {
    id: 'sop-escalation-matrix',
    title: 'Who to contact for what (escalation matrix)',
    domainId: 'comms-knowledge',
    summary: 'The right team and SLA for common store exceptions.',
    keywords: ['escalation', 'who to contact', 'contact', 'matrix', 'help', 'support', 'raise'],
    steps: [
      'Tills, handhelds, network, passwords → IT Service Desk (4h SLA).',
      'Fridges, heating, lighting, leaks → Facilities (24h SLA, sooner if safety).',
      'Out-of-stock with none on order → Stock / Supply team (8h SLA).',
      'People, pay, absence, conduct → HR / Regional Manager.',
      'Theft, aggression, shrink → Loss Prevention (immediate if in progress).',
    ],
    sourceId: 'src-requirements',
  },
  {
    id: 'sop-care-plan',
    title: 'Explain care & protection plan benefits',
    domainId: 'returns-service',
    summary: 'Talking points to attach a care plan at the point of sale.',
    keywords: ['care plan', 'protection', 'warranty', 'attach', 'cover', 'knowhow'],
    steps: [
      'Lead with peace of mind: accidental damage and breakdown cover beyond the guarantee.',
      'Mention fast repair or replacement and no-quibble support.',
      'Tailor to the product (e.g. screen damage for laptops/phones, parts & labour for appliances).',
      'Offer monthly or one-off payment options.',
    ],
    sourceId: 'src-currys',
  },
  {
    id: 'sop-opening',
    title: 'Store opening checklist',
    domainId: 'opening-closing',
    summary: 'The safe-start routine before the doors open.',
    keywords: ['opening', 'open up', 'morning', 'checklist', 'alarm', 'safety walk'],
    steps: [
      'Disarm the alarm and complete the lone-working check-in.',
      'Walk the floor for safety hazards and overnight issues.',
      'Power up tills, kiosks and the demo units; verify the network is up.',
      'Check the cash office float and set up tills.',
      'Brief the team on today’s priorities and promos before opening the doors.',
    ],
    sourceId: 'src-argos',
  },
  {
    id: 'sop-redeploy',
    title: 'Real-time redeployment during a peak',
    domainId: 'scheduling',
    summary: 'Covering an absence or a queue surge by moving colleagues to where demand is.',
    keywords: ['redeploy', 'redeployment', 'absence', 'cover', 'queue', 'peak', 'staffing', 'move colleague'],
    steps: [

      'Identify a colleague with the right skills who can move without leaving a gap.',
      'Brief them on the move and the priority (e.g. man the TV wall during the promo).',
      'Update the rota so the change is visible to the team and region.',
    ],
    sourceId: 'src-quorso',
  },
]

export function searchSops(query: string): Sop[] {
  const q = query.toLowerCase()
  const terms = q.split(/\s+/).filter(Boolean)
  return SOPS.map((sop) => {
    let score = 0
    for (const kw of sop.keywords) {
      if (q.includes(kw)) score += 3
    }
    for (const term of terms) {
      if (sop.title.toLowerCase().includes(term)) score += 2
      if (sop.keywords.some((kw) => kw.includes(term))) score += 1
      if (sop.summary.toLowerCase().includes(term)) score += 1
    }
    return { sop, score }
  })
    .filter((r) => r.score > 0)
    .sort((a, b) => b.score - a.score)
    .map((r) => r.sop)
}

data.reasoningText: I'm putting together a set of standard operating procedures for the Copilot's Ask skill, covering about 8-10 different scenarios across various domains like endcap displays, age-restricted item refusals, trade-ins, click and collect handovers, temperature logging, equipment faults, escalation protocols, care plan benefits, and store opening/closing procedures. Each SOP will include relevant keywords for searchability. Now I'm writing the TypeScript file to structure these knowledge cards.

arguments.content:
import type { Sop } from '@/types'

// Just-in-time SOP / knowledge cards. The Copilot "Ask" skill keyword-matches
// against these to return grounded answers during the demo.
export const SOPS: Sop[] = [
  {
    id: 'sop-endcap',
    title: 'Build & verify a promotional end-cap',
    domainId: 'merchandising',
    department: 'Gaming',
    summary: 'How to set up a promotional end-cap so it is compliant and selling within 30 minutes.',
    keywords: ['endcap', 'end cap', 'display', 'promo', 'merchandising', 'gondola', 'set up display', 'console bundle'],
    steps: [
      'Collect the promo kit (header card, shelf tickets, barker cards) from the comms folder.',
      'Clear the gondola end and clean the shelves before building.',
      'Place hero stock at eye level; ensure at least 6 facings of the bundle.',
      'Apply the bundle ticket and check the price matches the promo guidance.',
      'Power the demo unit and load the featured title/loop.',
      'Photograph the finished end-cap and mark the task complete to verify compliance.',
    ],
    sourceId: 'src-quorso',
  },
  {
    id: 'sop-age-restricted',
    title: 'Age-restricted sale & refusal process (Challenge 25)',
    domainId: 'loss-prevention',
    summary: 'Selling PEGI 18 games and other age-restricted lines, and how to log a refusal.',
    keywords: ['age', 'age restricted', 'pegi', 'pegi 18', 'challenge 25', 'refusal', 'id check', 'underage'],
    steps: [
      'If the customer looks under 25, ask for valid photo ID (passport, driving licence, PASS card).',
      'Check the date of birth confirms they are 18 or over for PEGI 18 titles.',
      'If ID is not provided or is invalid, politely refuse the sale.',
      'Log the refusal in the refusals register with time and reason.',
      'Never feel pressured — escalate aggression to the duty manager / security.',
    ],
    sourceId: 'src-requirements',
  },
  {
    id: 'sop-trade-in',
    title: 'Process a smartphone trade-in + cashback',
    domainId: 'returns-service',
    department: 'Mobile & Wearables',
    summary: 'Taking an old handset in trade and registering the cashback claim.',
    keywords: ['trade-in', 'trade in', 'cashback', 'phone', 'handset', 'part exchange'],
    steps: [
      'Run the device through the trade-in calculator on the kiosk to grade it.',
      'Confirm the customer has signed out of all accounts and removed the SIM/SD card.',
      'Factory-reset the device and verify it powers to setup screen.',
      'Apply the trade-in value against the new handset at the till.',
      'Help the customer scan the cashback QR card to register their claim.',
    ],
    sourceId: 'src-currys',
  },
  {
    id: 'sop-click-collect',
    title: 'Click & Collect handover and ID verification',
    domainId: 'fulfilment',
    department: 'Customer Service',
    summary: 'Handing over a collection order correctly, including ID checks for high-value items.',
    keywords: ['click and collect', 'click & collect', 'collection', 'handover', 'fast track', 'pick up', 'id verification'],
    steps: [
      'Ask for the order reference and the collector’s name.',
      'For high-value or age-restricted items, verify photo ID matches the order.',
      'Retrieve the order from the staging area and check the items against the pick list.',
      'Confirm the customer is happy, then scan to close the collection.',
      'Capture proof of collection (signature or scan) on the handheld.',
    ],
    sourceId: 'src-argos',
  },
  {
    id: 'sop-temperature-log',
    title: 'Stockroom temperature & equipment check',
    domainId: 'safety-compliance',
    summary: 'Daily temperature log for the stockroom chiller and the staff vending area.',
    keywords: ['temperature', 'temp log', 'fridge', 'chiller', 'compliance check', 'safety check'],
    steps: [
      'Read the chiller thermometer and record the temperature on the digital form.',
      'Acceptable range is 1°C to 5°C — if outside, raise an equipment fault immediately.',
      'Check the door seal and that nothing is blocking airflow.',
      'Sign and timestamp the log; the task captures this as evidence.',
    ],
    sourceId: 'src-axonify',
  },
  {
    id: 'sop-equipment-fault',
    title: 'Log an equipment / facilities fault',
    domainId: 'equipment-it',
    summary: 'Reporting a broken till, display fridge, printer or facilities issue to the right team.',
    keywords: ['fault', 'broken', 'repair', 'facilities', 'till down', 'printer', 'fridge', 'equipment', 'escalate'],
    steps: [
      'Make the area safe and put any affected equipment out of use.',
      'Open a fault ticket and select the category (IT for tills/printers, Facilities for fridges/heating/lighting).',
      'Add a short description and a photo of the issue.',
      'The system routes it to the right team with an SLA — track it on the Escalations board.',
    ],
    sourceId: 'src-axonify',
  },
  {
    id: 'sop-escalation-matrix',
    title: 'Who to contact for what (escalation matrix)',
    domainId: 'comms-knowledge',
    summary: 'The right team and SLA for common store exceptions.',
    keywords: ['escalation', 'who to contact', 'contact', 'matrix', 'help', 'support', 'raise'],
    steps: [
      'Tills, handhelds, network, passwords → IT Service Desk (4h SLA).',
      'Fridges, heating, lighting, leaks → Facilities (24h SLA, sooner if safety).',
      'Out-of-stock with none on order → Stock / Supply team (8h SLA).',
      'People, pay, absence, conduct → HR / Regional Manager.',
      'Theft, aggression, shrink → Loss Prevention (immediate if in progress).',
    ],
    sourceId: 'src-requirements',
  },
  {
    id: 'sop-care-plan',
    title: 'Explain care & protection plan benefits',
    domainId: 'returns-service',
    summary: 'Talking points to attach a care plan at the point of sale.',
    keywords: ['care plan', 'protection', 'warranty', 'attach', 'cover', 'knowhow'],
    steps: [
      'Lead with peace of mind: accidental damage and breakdown cover beyond the guarantee.',
      'Mention fast repair or replacement and no-quibble support.',
      'Tailor to the product (e.g. screen damage for laptops/phones, parts & labour for appliances).',
      'Offer monthly or one-off payment options.',
    ],
    sourceId: 'src-currys',
  },
  {
    id: 'sop-opening',
    title: 'Store opening checklist',
    domainId: 'opening-closing',
    summary: 'The safe-start routine before the doors open.',
    keywords: ['opening', 'open up', 'morning', 'checklist', 'alarm', 'safety walk'],
    steps: [
      'Disarm the alarm and complete the lone-working check-in.',
      'Walk the floor for safety hazards and overnight issues.',
      'Power up tills, kiosks and the demo units; verify the network is up.',
      'Check the cash office float and set up tills.',
      'Brief the team on today’s priorities and promos before opening the doors.',
    ],
    sourceId: 'src-argos',
  },
  {
    id: 'sop-redeploy',
    title: 'Real-time redeployment during a peak',
    domainId: 'scheduling',
    summary: 'Covering an absence or a queue surge by moving colleagues to where demand is.',
    keywords: ['redeploy', 'redeployment', 'absence', 'cover', 'queue', 'peak', 'staffing', 'move colleague'],
    steps: [
      'Check live cover by department against expected footfall.',
      'Identify a colleague with the right skills who can move without leaving a gap.',
      'Brief them on the move and the priority (e.g. man the TV wall during the promo).',
      'Update the rota so the change is visible to the team and region.',
    ],
    sourceId: 'src-quorso',
  },
]

export function searchSops(query: string): Sop[] {
  const q = query.toLowerCase()
  const terms = q.split(/\s+/).filter(Boolean)
  return SOPS.map((sop) => {
    let score = 0
    for (const kw of sop.keywords) {
      if (q.includes(kw)) score += 3
    }
    for (const term of terms) {
      if (sop.title.toLowerCase().includes(term)) score += 2
      if (sop.keywords.some((kw) => kw.includes(term))) score += 1
      if (sop.summary.toLowerCase().includes(term)) score += 1
    }
    return { sop, score }
  })
    .filter((r) => r.score > 0)
    .sort((a, b) => b.score - a.score)
    .map((r) => r.sop)
}

Now I'm setting up the signal definitions that the engine will convert into tasks, mapping out a comprehensive set across Store 214 that covers promotional issues, display verification gaps, attachment rates for specific products, staffing constraints, equipment maintenance, compliance deadlines, inventory replenishment needs, and training requirements.

I'll also include signals for other stores like expiring inventory at Zara locations, age-restricted item refusals, price discrepancies, critical out-of-stock situations on console bundles and AC units with no incoming orders, and OLED TV shortages with expected restocks. I'm creating around 18-22 signals total with deterministic IDs and timestamps anchored to the current demo time.

Writing signals.ts...

I'm thinking severity gets weighted 1-4 (low to critical), impact gets weighted based on GBP thresholds, and I'll add a deadline bonus to push urgent items up. The scoring function will take severity, estimated impact, and due date, then return both the raw score and the band assignment.

toolRequests.name: create_file

data.reasoningText:
Now I'm tackling the signalsEngine.ts file, which transforms signals into tasks using transparent rules where each rule generates a Task. I need to set up the imports for all the necessary types and utility functions like domain lookups, product data, promotions, store information, colleague details, and priority computation.

I'm building a helper function to create tasks that handles common fields and automatically calculates priority and due dates. Then I'll define a rule registry where each signal type maps to a function that returns the partial task configuration including title, suggested action, evidence requirements, steps, and other metadata. For each signal type—PromoUnderperforming, DisplayUnverified, and LowAttachRate—I'm specifying the exact task details like what needs to be done, what evidence to collect, and which source system it comes from.

Now I'm adding more signal types. PriceMismatch is straightforward: reprint and verify the shelf ticket. OutOfStock is more complex—it branches based on whether stock is on order. If nothing's on order during a live promo, it escalates to the Stock team with an 8-hour SLA. If stock is available in the back or on order, the task is just to replenish the floor. I'm checking the logic against the store 214 fan example: it has 2 on hand and 20 on order, so it takes the replenish path rather than the escalation path.

Looking at the other cases: store 301 console bundle and 309 AC both have zero on hand and zero on order, so they escalate. Store 126 TV has zero on hand but 4 units on order, which technically fits the replenish path but feels slightly off since there's nothing to fill from the stockroom yet. I'm refining the OutOfStock logic to handle this better—if stock is on order but not yet arrived, the task should be to bridge the gap by confirming the delivery ETA and offering alternatives, rather than asking someone to replenish from an empty stockroom.

Now I'm defining the other action types: ClickCollectAgeing handles orders waiting too long in the collection area, ColleagueAbsence triggers redeployment when someone's out, TrainingExpiring reminds about certification renewals, and EquipmentFault routes repairs to either Facilities or IT depending on whether it's a temperature/chiller issue or an IT device problem.

For ComplianceDue, I'm parsing the message to determine if it's a temperature log or fire safety check, then setting the priority based on severity. AgeRestrictedRefusal logs refusals in the register, NegativeFeedback triggers service recovery follow-ups, and I'm including ShrinkAnomaly for completeness even though it's not currently in the signals.

Most tasks start as not_started, but the escalated ones are OutOfStock-none-on-order and EquipmentFault. I'm thinking through the demo flow: the store 214 fridge fault should start escalated since it's high severity, showing the escalation board immediately with a 24-hour Facilities SLA. The store 301 out-of-stock bundle also starts escalated to Stock with an 8-hour SLA. This lets the store manager focus on the promo end-cap task during the demo, which moves from not_started to complete with evidence.

I'm adding initial thread updates for context—for the OOS task, a note that it was raised to Stock with no inventory on order, and for the fridge, a note that it was logged via handheld with the area secured. Now I'm building the task engine: I'll generate deterministic task IDs from signal IDs, set createdAt from the signal's detectedAt, and calculate dueAt relative to the demo time so countdowns feel realistic. The owner is the store manager, and I'm computing priority from severity, impact, and due date. I'll also create a helper to build tasks and map rules to descriptions so the Signals Explorer can explain the reasoning behind each task.

data.reasoningText:
I'm setting up the analytics modules now—estateHealth.ts will compute a composite score from completion rates, compliance, and stock availability metrics, while also identifying stores at risk. The kpi.ts file will handle aggregation helpers, and impact.ts will track changes since the morning.

For the estate health calculation, I'm weighting the components differently to avoid penalizing the initial state where most tasks haven't started yet. The base score will lean more heavily on the seeded KPI data (compliance, stock availability, and CSAT) rather than task completion, then apply a penalty factor for overdue or at-risk tasks to keep the starting health around 82.

To show impact over the course of the demo, I'll compare the current KPI values against the morning snapshot stored in the data—that baseline difference already shows positive movement for some metrics. When tasks complete, especially compliance-related ones, I can further improve the health score through the impact module.

For the impact display itself, I'll track a few key metrics: the count of completed tasks, the total estimated financial risk mitigated from those completions, and a synthetic compliance lift calculated from compliance and safety-focused tasks. To give the demo a starting point with non-zero impact even before anything is completed, I'll seed a few tasks as already done earlier in the day.

I'm going to consolidate the analytics logic into a single engine/analytics.ts file rather than splitting across multiple files. I'll build helper functions for averaging metrics, calculating estate health scores, and breaking down pillar health based on task completion and exceptions.

Writing analytics functions... Implementing the health scoring formulas now: each pillar gets a base score (people 88, trading 79, stock 77, risk 90, enablement 85) that adjusts down for open exceptions and up for completed tasks, clamped between 40-99. The estate health score is the average across all pillars. For stores at risk, I'm identifying those with compliance below 70% or open critical tasks—that gives me around 5 stores (126, 228, 219, 309, 301) which I'll tighten to match the "3 stores at risk" narrative. Now I'm building the analytics module to track impact since morning—calculating completed tasks, the compliance lift they've generated, and how many stores have moved out of risk as actions get resolved. I'll compute the morning baseline compliance around 79% and show how completed trading and risk tasks incrementally improve that figure. I'll seed a few completed tasks at initialization to establish a baseline—marking a couple of signal-derived tasks as done so there's a non-zero morning count. Then I'll create an analytics function that tracks the four key metrics: actions completed, risk mitigated in pounds, compliance shift from morning to now, and open exceptions, letting the UI decide which to display. I'll compute compliance by adding a baseline of 3 to the morning score of 79, then increment it further based on completed compliance-related tasks, clamped to 0-100. For open exceptions, I'll start with a morning baseline of 9 and show the current count of escalated tasks (around 3 at seed), which reads as improvement as the user resolves them. Now I'll implement the analytics engine to tie all these metrics together.

{"content": "import type { Priority, Task, TaskTemplate } from '@/types'
import { SIGNALS } from '@/data/signals'
import { buildTasksFromSignals } from '@/engine/signalsEngine'
import { TASK_TEMPLATE_BY_ID } from '@/data/taskTemplates'
import { DOMAIN_BY_ID, pillarOfDomain } from '@/data/domains'
import { managerOfStore } from '@/data/stores'
import { computePriority } from '@/engine/priority'
import { DUE_HOURS_BY_SEVERITY } from '@/engine/priority'
import { fromNow, hoursFromNow } from '@/data/now'
import type { Severity } from '@/types'

/** Bump to invalidate persisted demo state when the seed changes. */
export const SEED_VERSION = 4

let manualSeq = 0

interface InstantiateOpts {
  id?: string
  source?: Task['source']
  status?: Task['status']
  priority?: Priority
  severity?: Severity
  estImpactGBP?: number
  rationale?: string
  suggestedAction?: string
  dueInHours?: number
  completedMinsAgo?: number
  ownerUserId?: string
}

export function instantiateTemplate(
  template: TaskTemplate,
  storeId: string,
  opts: InstantiateOpts = {},
): Task {
  const domain = DOMAIN_BY_ID[template.domainId]
  const pillar = pillarOfDomain(template.domainId)
  const severity: Severity = opts.severity ?? 'medium'
  const estImpactGBP = opts.estImpactGBP ?? 0
  const dueAt = hoursFromNow(opts.dueInHours ?? DUE_HOURS_BY_SEVERITY[severity])
  const { score, band } = computePriority(severity, estImpactGBP, dueAt)
  const owner = opts.ownerUserId ?? managerOfStore(storeId)?.id ?? 'u-hq'
  const id = opts.id ?? `task-tmpl-${template.id}-${storeId}-${manualSeq++}`
  const completed = opts.completedMinsAgo != null
  void domain

  return {
    id,
    title: template.title,
    rationale: opts.rationale ?? `Standing ${template.recurring ?? 'operational'} task.`,
    suggestedAction: opts.suggestedAction ?? 'Complete the steps and mark done.',
    source: opts.source ?? 'template',
    domainId: template.domainId,
    pillarId: pillar.id,
    priority: opts.priority ?? band,
    priorityScore: score,
    status: completed ? 'complete' : opts.status ?? 'not_started',
    storeId,
    ownerUserId: owner,
    dueAt,
    createdAt: fromNow(-240),
    estImpactGBP,
    evidenceRequired: template.evidenceRequired,
    steps: template.steps.map((s, i) => ({
      id: `${id}-s${i}`,
      label: s.label,
      type: s.type,
      done: completed,
    })),
    evidence: [],
    completedAt: completed ? fromNow(-(opts.completedMinsAgo ?? 0)) : undefined,
  }
}

/** Build the full initial task list for the demo estate. */
export function buildSeedTasks(): Task[] {
  manualSeq = 0
  const aiTasks = buildTasksFromSignals(SIGNALS)

  // A few standing/manual tasks so the Store views feel like a real day.
  const standing: Task[] = [
    instantiateTemplate(TASK_TEMPLATE_BY_ID['tt-opening'], 's-214', {
      id: 'task-214-opening',
      completedMinsAgo: 180,
      rationale: 'Opening routine completed by the duty manager this morning.',
    }),
    instantiateTemplate(TASK_TEMPLATE_BY_ID['tt-huddle'], 's-214', {
      id: 'task-214-huddle',
      completedMinsAgo: 165,
      rationale: 'Start-of-shift huddle held — today’s promos and priorities shared.',
    }),
    instantiateTemplate(TASK_TEMPLATE_BY_ID['tt-closing'], 's-214', {
      id: 'task-214-closing',
      rationale: 'Closing routine due at the end of trade.',
      dueInHours: 9,
    }),
    instantiateTemplate(TASK_TEMPLATE_BY_ID['tt-policy-ack'], 's-214', {
      id: 'task-214-policy',
      source: 'manual',
      rationale: 'HQ published an updated returns & refunds policy — please read and acknowledge.',
      suggestedAction: 'Read the updated policy and confirm understood.',
      priority: 'P3',
    }),
  ]

  return [...aiTasks, ...standing]
}

Now I'm setting up the Zustand store to manage the core state—persona data with role, user ID, and store ID, a tasks array seeded from the engine, and action handlers for completing steps, capturing evidence, managing tasks, escalations, and deployment. I'll also track UI state for the copilot and sources panels, with selectors and derived values computed as needed in components.

For persistence, I'm storing tasks, persona, and UI flags in localStorage with versioning and migration logic, so on reset the store rebuilds from seed data. I'm thinking through what to expose—getTasks(), tasksForStore() filtered by store ID, and the current persona's scope—keeping the store lean and computing derived state where it's needed rather than duplicating it.

Now I'm mapping out the action signatures: setPersona and setActiveStore for navigation, toggleStep and setStepValue for task editing, addEvidence for capturing details, completeTask and startTask for status changes, escalateTask and related escalation actions for handling issues, plus createTask and reassignTask for manual operations. I'm also adding resetDemo to clear everything and rebuild from seed, and UI toggles for the copilot and sources panels.

I'll organize the seed data in a separate file to keep the store clean. I'm creating a buildSeedTasks function that generates the initial task set from the signal data plus some standing tasks like opening and closing checklists, and a manual task from HQ about the returns policy. I'll use a helper to instantiate tasks from templates so I can reuse the structure across stores.

import type { Priority, Task, TaskTemplate } from '@/types'
import { SIGNALS } from '@/data/signals'
import { buildTasksFromSignals } from '@/engine/signalsEngine'
import { TASK_TEMPLATE_BY_ID } from '@/data/taskTemplates'
import { DOMAIN_BY_ID, pillarOfDomain } from '@/data/domains'
import { managerOfStore } from '@/data/stores'
import { computePriority } from '@/engine/priority'
import { DUE_HOURS_BY_SEVERITY } from '@/engine/priority'
import { fromNow, hoursFromNow } from '@/data/now'
import type { Severity } from '@/types'

/** Bump to invalidate persisted demo state when the seed changes. */
export const SEED_VERSION = 4

let manualSeq = 0

interface InstantiateOpts {
  id?: string
  source?: Task['source']
  status?: Task['status']
  priority?: Priority
  severity?: Severity
  estImpactGBP?: number
  rationale?: string
  suggestedAction?: string
  dueInHours?: number
  completedMinsAgo?: number
  ownerUserId?: string
}

export function instantiateTemplate(
  template: TaskTemplate,
  storeId: string,
  opts: InstantiateOpts = {},
): Task {
  const domain = DOMAIN_BY_ID[template.domainId]
  const pillar = pillarOfDomain(template.domainId)
  const severity: Severity = opts.severity ?? 'medium'
  const estImpactGBP = opts.estImpactGBP ?? 0
  const dueAt = hoursFromNow(opts.dueInHours ?? DUE_HOURS_BY_SEVERITY[severity])
  const { score, band } = computePriority(severity, estImpactGBP, dueAt)
  const owner = opts.ownerUserId ?? managerOfStore(storeId)?.id ?? 'u-hq'
  const id = opts.id ?? `task-tmpl-${template.id}-${storeId}-${manualSeq++}`
  const completed = opts.completedMinsAgo != null
  void domain

  return {
    id,
    title: template.title,
    rationale: opts.rationale ?? `Standing ${template.recurring ?? 'operational'} task.`,
    suggestedAction: opts.suggestedAction ?? 'Complete the steps and mark done.',
    source: opts.source ?? 'template',
    domainId: template.domainId,
    pillarId: pillar.id,
    priority: opts.priority ?? band,
    priorityScore: score,
    status: completed ? 'complete' : opts.status ?? 'not_started',
    storeId,
    ownerUserId: owner,
    dueAt,
    createdAt: fromNow(-240),
    estImpactGBP,
    evidenceRequired: template.evidenceRequired,
    steps: template.steps.map((s, i) => ({
      id: `${id}-s${i}`,
      label: s.label,
      type: s.type,
      done: completed,
    })),
    evidence: [],
    completedAt: completed ? fromNow(-(opts.completedMinsAgo ?? 0)) : undefined,
  }
}

/** Build the full initial task list for the demo estate. */
export function buildSeedTasks(): Task[] {
  manualSeq = 0
  const aiTasks = buildTasksFromSignals(SIGNALS)

  // A few standing/manual tasks so the Store views feel like a real day.
  const standing: Task[] = [
    instantiateTemplate(TASK_TEMPLATE_BY_ID['tt-opening'], 's-214', {
      id: 'task-214-opening',
      completedMinsAgo: 180,
      rationale: 'Opening routine completed by the duty manager this morning.',
    }),
    instantiateTemplate(TASK_TEMPLATE_BY_ID['tt-huddle'], 's-214', {
      id: 'task-214-huddle',
      completedMinsAgo: 165,
      rationale: 'Start-of-shift huddle held — today’s promos and priorities shared.',
    }),
    instantiateTemplate(TASK_TEMPLATE_BY_ID['tt-closing'], 's-214', {
      id: 'task-214-closing',
      rationale: 'Closing routine due at the end of trade.',
      dueInHours: 9,
    }),
    instantiateTemplate(TASK_TEMPLATE_BY_ID['tt-policy-ack'], 's-214', {
      id: 'task-214-policy',
      source: 'manual',
      rationale: 'HQ published an updated returns & refunds policy — please read and acknowledge.',
      suggestedAction: 'Read the updated policy and confirm understood.',
      priority: 'P3',
    }),
  ]

  return [...aiTasks, ...standing]
}

Now I'm setting up the layout components—a device frame wrapper for the tablet views, a role-aware sidebar that changes based on user permissions, a top bar with persona switching and controls, and a main shell that ties everything together. The navigation structure varies by role, so HQ users see different menu items than other personas.

For the top bar, I'm placing the logo on the left, a segmented control in the center to switch between HQ/Regional/Store roles, and action buttons on the right for the copilot, sources, and demo reset. The persona switcher will trigger navigation to the appropriate role home.

The device frame will constrain the width and add chrome styling—for store views I'm using a wider tablet frame with a status bar showing the time and store name to simulate an in-store handheld experience.

For the overall layout, I'm keeping it flexible: AppShell always renders the top bar, then for Store persona I'll center a DeviceFrame containing the tablet with its own internal navigation tabs, while HQ and Regional get the traditional left sidebar plus main content area. This gives the store views that authentic mobile-app feel while keeping the control tower layout for the other roles.

I'll start by building out DeviceFrame as a pure visual wrapper component, then layer in the Store-specific layout logic and navigation tabs on top of it.

data.reasoningText:
I'm building out the main CopilotPanel component with four skill tabs—Prioritise, Ask, Recommend, and Explain. The Prioritise tab will display ranked open tasks differently depending on the context: top tasks across the entire estate for HQ, regional tasks for regional users, or store-specific tasks for store-level access. I'll show the top five as compact cards with priority level, title, impact, and a button to navigate to the full task details.

For the Ask tab, I'm adding an input field with suggestion chips that searches SOPs and returns the top result with steps and related items. The Recommend tab will have similar input with example chips, calling a product recommendation function that matches items by price and stock status, then shows reasons and care plan options. The Explain tab will document how the prioritization algorithm works and list out the signal-type rules.

I'll use useNavigate for routing and set the active store ID to default to s-214 when not in a store context, so HQ and regional users can still access recommendations. Each tab will be its own subcomponent within the file, and I need to manage controlled inputs carefully with useState.

import { useNavigate } from 'react-router-dom' import type { Task } from '@/types' import { STORES, STORE_BY_ID, REGION_BY_ID } from '@/data/stores' import { KPI_BY_STORE } from '@/data/kpis' import { tasksForStore, openExceptions } from '@/store/selectors' import { Table, TableBody, TableCell, TableHead, TableHeader, TableRow } from '@/components/ui/table' import { ProgressBar } from '@/components/shared/Stat' import { cn } from '@/lib/utils' import { ChevronRight } from 'lucide-react' export interface LeagueRow { storeId: string score: number } export function storeScore(storeId: string): number { const kpi = KPI_BY_STORE[storeId] return Math.round(0.5 * kpi.compliancePct + 0.3 * Math.min(100, kpi.salesVsTargetPct) + 0.2 * (100 - kpi.oosRatePct * 4)) } export function StoreLeagueTable({ tasks, storeIds, limit, }: { tasks: Task[] storeIds?: string[] limit?: number }) { const navigate = useNavigate() const ids = storeIds ?? STORES.map((s) => s.id) const rows = ids .map((storeId) => ({ storeId, score: storeScore(storeId) })) .sort((a, b) => b.score - a.score) .slice(0, limit) return (
# Store Region Compliance Sales Open Exceptions {rows.map((row, i) => { const store = STORE_BY_ID[row.storeId] const kpi = KPI_BY_STORE[row.storeId] const st = tasksForStore(tasks, row.storeId) const open = st.filter((t) => t.status !== 'complete').length const exceptions = openExceptions(st).length return ( navigate(`/region/store/${row.storeId}`)} > {i + 1}

) }

data.reasoningText:
Now I'm building the Campaign Centre view that displays how a specific promotion is performing across the store network. It pulls the promotion ID from the URL and loads a default bundle if none is specified. The page shows the campaign header with key details like the promotion name, mechanics, dates and target uplift, followed by KPI cards tracking stores live, compliance percentage, adoption rate and sales lift. Below that is a compliance heatmap that maps stores against execution checks to visualize where the promotion is being executed properly.

I'm determining the compliance checks deterministically based on store signals and inventory data. For each store, I evaluate whether the display is built, if it's ticketed, whether the demo is running, and if stock is available. Store 214 has an unverified display signal so that check fails, store 228 has low compliance, and store 301 is out of stock. The heatmap cells color-code as green, amber, or red based on these results. I'm also building a sales lift chart that compares actual uplift against targets by region, and a rollup section to surface any stores experiencing issues with this promotion.

Building the Store Drilldown view now—I'll pull the storeId from the route params and display the store's header with name, region, and format, then show KPI stats for compliance, sales, attach, out-of-stock, and CSAT. Below that I'm adding a compact list of open tasks and an exceptions inbox scoped to that specific store, with an action button to switch the persona and set the active store.

I'm thinking of adding a dedicated `enterStore(storeId)` action to the store that handles all three updates together, rather than trying to chain existing actions. Let me add that to the store.

import { HashRouter, Routes, Route, Navigate } from 'react-router-dom'
import { AppShell } from '@/components/layout/AppShell'
import { useAppStore } from '@/store/useAppStore'
import { DailyBrief } from '@/pages/store/DailyBrief'
import { TaskDetail } from '@/pages/store/TaskDetail'
import { Checklists } from '@/pages/store/Checklists'
import { Workforce } from '@/pages/store/Workforce'
import { Knowledge } from '@/pages/store/Knowledge'
import { Assist } from '@/pages/store/Assist'
import { ControlTower } from '@/pages/hq/ControlTower'
import { CampaignCentre } from '@/pages/hq/CampaignCentre'
import { Analytics } from '@/pages/hq/Analytics'
import { SignalsExplorer } from '@/pages/hq/SignalsExplorer'
import { RegionCockpit } from '@/pages/region/Cockpit'
import { StoreDrilldown } from '@/pages/region/StoreDrilldown'
import { Escalations } from '@/pages/region/Escalations'
import { DomainCatalogue } from '@/pages/DomainCatalogue'
import { CreateTask } from '@/pages/CreateTask'
import { Impact } from '@/pages/Impact'

function PersonaHome() {
  const role = useAppStore((s) => s.role)
  const to = role === 'HQ' ? '/hq' : role === 'Regional' ? '/region' : '/store'
  return <Navigate to={to} replace />
}

export default function App() {
  return (
    <HashRouter>

}

{"content": "# wattsRus — Store Operations Copilot

A self-contained **demo** of a retail store operations management platform for a fictional
UK electronics & gaming retailer, **wattsRus**. It shows how a "Store Operations Copilot"
turns operational signals into **prioritised daily actions**, tracks execution with
**proof/evidence**, and **escalates exceptions** across HQ, regional and store roles.

> 100% synthetic data · no backend · runs entirely in the browser (offline-capable SPA).

## Run it

```bash
npm install
npm run dev      # http://localhost:5173
npm run build    # production build to /dist
npm run preview  # serve the production build
```

## What's inside

- **Three personas** (switch in the top bar):
  - **HQ / Central Ops** — Estate Control Tower, Campaign Command Centre, Analytics, Signals Explorer.
  - **Regional Manager** — Store Cockpit (league table), Escalations & SLAs, store drill-down.
  - **Store / Colleague** — in-store tablet: Daily Brief, Task detail + evidence, Checklists,
    Workforce, Knowledge, and Customer Assist (clienteling).
- **wattsRus Copilot** — one assistant, five skills: **Prioritise**, **Ask** (SOP knowledge),
  **Recommend** (product clienteling), **Explain** (Signals Explorer), reachable from the launcher.
- **Operating model** — the **14 store task domains** grouped into **5 pillars** (People & Workforce,
  Trading & Execution, Stock & Fulfilment, Risk/Safety/Compliance, Enablement & Support).
- **Sources panel** — lists the public category research the generic requirements are based on
  (Axonify, Quorso, Currys, Argos, plus named category leaders), so the demo is *based on public
  research, not copied from any single vendor*.
- **Reset demo** button restores the seeded start-of-day state for a clean re-run.

## The scenario — "a busy Saturday in June"

Multiple live promotions (console bundle, Big Match TV event, back-to-school laptops, phone
trade-in + cashback, summer cooling) and live operational signals across 12 stores / 3 regions
exercise ~10 of the 14 domains: promo execution, stock/replenishment, click & collect, a colleague
absence + redeployment, an equipment fault, compliance checks, an age-restricted refusal, and a
clienteling request.

## 15-minute talk track

1. **(2m) HQ Control Tower** — estate health 82; pillar tiles show **Trading & Stock dipping**;
   live signals feed lighting up; 4 stores need attention.
2. **(2m) Signals Explorer** — *"here's why the Copilot prioritised these"* — show a rule turning a
   promo + unverified-display signal into a ranked, evidence-required task (trust / explainability).
3. **(4m) Store tablet (#214 Manchester)** — Daily Brief priorities ranked across domains. Open the
   top promo task → work the checklist → **take a photo + capture a count** → **Mark complete** →
   "£3,200 protected" toast, list re-ranks, KPIs move.
4. **(2m) Copilot Assist (clienteling)** — ask *"laptop for a student doing video editing, ~£700"* →
   in-stock matches + accessory/care-plan attach. "The same Copilot that runs your day helps you sell."
5. **(2m) Exception** — the stockroom chiller fault and a console-bundle OOS-with-none-on-order
   **auto-escalate** (Facilities / Stock) with **SLA countdowns**; the colleague absence triggers a
   **redeployment** suggestion.
6. **(3m) Regional cockpit + Impact** — league table, exceptions/SLA inbox, **nudge** a lagging
   store; then **Impact since morning** (actions done, compliance ↑, £ risk mitigated). Open the
   **Sources** panel to close on "built on public category research."

## Tech

Vite · React 19 · TypeScript · React Router (hash) · Zustand (persisted) · Tailwind CSS v4 ·
shadcn/ui · Recharts. The "AI" is a transparent, deterministic rules engine over the seed data —
no network calls, so it is reliable offline and on stage.

## Project structure

```
src/
  data/        synthetic estate: domains, stores, products, promotions, inventory,
               KPIs, colleagues/shifts, signals, SOPs, sources, seed
  engine/      signals→tasks rules, priority, SLA, estate-health & impact analytics
  copilot/     clienteling recommender (Ask/Explain use data + engine)
  store/       Zustand store + selectors
  components/  layout, shared badges/stats, task, estate, copilot, sources, ui (shadcn)
  pages/       store/ · region/ · hq/ · shared (domains, create task, impact)
```

All data is fictional and for illustration only.

data.content:
The wattsRus Store Operations Copilot is built, verified, and running. Here's what's in place.

## Built & working
A complete offline React SPA (Vite + React 19 + TS + Tailwind v4 + shadcn/ui + Zustand + Recharts) with **zero backend** and all synthetic data. The dev server is running at **http://localhost:5173**.

**Three personas** (top-bar switcher): HQ, Regional, Store — each with its own navigation and views.

**The 14 task domains → 5 pillars** are the data spine; every signal, task, KPI and SOP is tagged to a domain.

**wattsRus Copilot, 5 skills** — Prioritise, Ask (SOP knowledge), **Recommend (clienteling/product recommendations)**, Explain (Signals Explorer), all deterministic over seed data.

## Verified live in the browser
- **Store tablet flow (the "aha"):** Daily Brief → open P1 task → tick steps → **capture photo + count evidence** → Mark complete → *"£3,200 protected"* toast → list re-ranks 12→11 and rolls up to Region/HQ.
- **HQ Control Tower:** estate health **82**, pillar tiles showing **Trading & Stock dipping**, live signals feed, league table.
- **Region Cockpit:** league table + **SLA exception inbox** (chiller fault → Facilities, 23h 20m left) with nudge/view.
- **Copilot clienteling:** *"laptop for a student doing video editing, ~£700"* → CreatorBook 15 best match, in-stock matches, accessory + care-plan attach.

## Also included
- **Campaign Centre** (compliance heatmap + sales-lift chart), **Analytics** (trend charts + coaching), **Signals Explorer** (signal→rule→task), **Domain Catalogue** (all 14), **Create Task**, **Impact since morning**, and the **Sources panel** (Axonify/Quorso/Currys/Argos + leaders) so you can say it's built on public research.
- **Reset demo** button for clean re-runs; deterministic clock ("busy Saturday in June") so SLAs/impact always read right on stage.
- `README.md` with run steps and the **15-minute talk track**.

Typecheck and production build are both clean. GitHub Pages is deferred per your note — but `base: './'` + HashRouter are already set so deployment later is a one-step `npm run build`.

To view it now: the server is live, or run `npm run dev`. Reset between rehearsals with the top-bar **Reset** button.

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking for three things: a guided tour feature to walk through the app's flows automatically, PWA setup so it can be installed on phones (which isn't currently configured), and clarification on whether they actually need the tablet outline for the colleague store view.

For the admin panel, they want to change branding on the fly during demos — uploading logos and setting names dynamically, with defaults stored on GitHub. Since this is a static SPA with no backend, the realistic approach is to store brand presets in localStorage for persistence across sessions, while shipping default presets (like wattsRus) as version-controlled files in the repo. The admin view would let users create and manage these locally stored brands.

I could also add an export/import feature so they can save custom brands as JSON and commit them back to the repo if they want to make them permanent defaults — that bridges the gap between runtime uploads and the "stored on GitHub" requirement nicely.

For the PWA side, I need to set up a manifest file with the app name, icons, and theme color to make it installable.  The tricky part is that PWA icons are static at install time, so while the in-app branding will change dynamically based on the active brand stored in localStorage, the installed app icon will stay fixed to the default wattsRus logo. I'll need to generate icon assets (192px, 512px, and maskable variants) and use vite-plugin-pwa with Workbox to auto-generate the service worker and manifest — that handles offline caching and all the PWA boilerplate cleanly.

For the icons themselves, I'm weighing options: I could use sharp with a Node script to rasterize an SVG into PNGs, but that adds build complexity and potential Windows compatibility issues. Alternatively, I could rely on SVG icons in the manifest (modern Chrome supports this now), but iOS and Android still prefer PNG formats, especially for the apple-touch-icon. The safest approach for a solid demo is probably to create the PNG icons upfront — either by generating them with sharp during the build, or by hand-authoring simple placeholder PNGs and including them in the public folder. I'll create the SVG source, run a sharp script to generate the PNGs into `/public`, and let vite-plugin-pwa reference them as committed assets. I'm sticking with vite-plugin-pwa since it handles service worker generation and update prompts cleanly.

For the device frame, I'll make it responsive: on narrow viewports or when running as an installed PWA, drop the bezel and go full-screen like a native app, but keep the frame on desktop so it reads as an in-store device mock during presentations.

For the tour, I'm thinking an auto-play guided demo mode that scripts a sequence of steps—each with a route, optional selector, caption, and action—that navigates automatically, highlights elements with a spotlight overlay, and can trigger store actions directly rather than fragile DOM clicks. I'll add play/pause/next/prev controls and keyboard navigation.

The key is anchoring highlights to stable `data-tour` attributes on key UI elements (persona switcher, task cards, copilot launcher, etc.) so the tour reliably finds and spotlights them as it progresses through the flows. I'll build a `TourProvider` and overlay component to handle the spotlight rendering and auto-advance timing, then add a "Guided demo" button to the top bar.

Beyond the tour, there are some nice polish touches I could add—keyboard shortcuts, animated route transitions, count-up animations on KPIs, confetti on task completion, or a PWA install button—but the tour itself is the main priority.

For the PWA and branding system, I'm thinking a `BrandProvider` that manages the active brand and custom uploads, with a `/admin` page to switch between presets (wattsRus default plus a couple examples), upload logos as data URLs to localStorage, and optionally tweak the accent color live. The brand name and logo appear in the top bar, and I'll wire the accent color to a CSS variable so changes are instant. For GitHub storage, the defaults stay committed in `src/data/brands.ts` while user uploads live in localStorage, with an export-to-commit workflow explained in the UI.

On device frames, I'll make the tablet frame disappear on mobile and in standalone mode, so the app goes full-bleed on phones but stays framed on desktop.

I'm planning a separate `useBrandStore` in Zustand so brand state persists independently of demo resets—that way resetting the demo doesn't wipe the user's branding choices. The brand model will have an id, name, accent color, and a logoType field to distinguish between the default icon and uploaded images, with an optional logoDataUrl for custom uploads and a builtIn flag to prevent deletion of defaults.

I'll seed the store with wattsRus as the default (with the Zap icon and #464feb accent) plus a couple of example built-in brands like "Currents" and "PowerHub" with different accent colors so users can immediately see the switching in action. Built-in brands stay protected as fallbacks, but custom ones are deletable. At runtime, I'll apply the selected brand's accent by setting CSS custom properties like `--primary`, `--ring`, and `--sidebar-primary`, and update the PWA theme-color meta tag. In the TopBar, the logo will render as an image if it's a custom upload, otherwise it'll show the Zap icon with the accent background.

For the manifest, I'll use "wattsRus · Store Ops" as the default name to keep it recognizable, though the installed PWA name is fixed at build time. I'm going to hand-roll the onboarding tour to have precise control over auto-play, and I'll do the same for the count-up animation with a small custom hook.

Now I need to set up the dependencies—vite-plugin-pwa for PWA support and a tool for generating the icon set. I'm leaning toward using @vite-pwa/assets-generator, which can take a single SVG and produce a full set of PNGs that integrate cleanly with the plugin.

I'll create a brand-source.svg with a 512 viewBox containing the brand accent rounded square and a white lightning bolt, then run the generator CLI to output the icons into the public folder. After that, I'll configure the plugin in vite.config.ts, register the service worker in main.tsx, add the iOS meta tags to index.html, and then wire up the branding throughout the app—the store context, defaults, the Admin page, and the TopBar integration.

I'm thinking through the service worker registration strategy: with a relative base and hash router, I'll set `registerType: 'prompt'` so I can show update toasts when a new version is available, giving users control over when to refresh.

I'll also add theme and background colors to the manifest using the brand purple and white, then tackle the icon generation with the assets generator tool—if it fails due to network or binary issues, I have a fallback plan to hand-create SVG icons instead since modern browsers support them in the manifest.

Now I'm ready to start the implementation. I'll install the PWA plugin and generator, hand-roll the tour and count-up features, and keep the build verification frequent since the PWA setup is the riskiest part.

The terminal being disabled means I can't install dependencies or run build tools, so I'll need to implement everything from scratch without external packages. I can hand-roll the PWA setup, icon generation, and configuration directly in the code.

For the service worker and manifest, I'll create them as plain files in the public directory and register them in the main app. Since I can't generate PNG icons, I'll use SVG icons instead—modern browsers support them in the manifest, and while older iOS doesn't support SVG for apple-touch-icon, it'll gracefully fall back to a page screenshot. The tricky part is that Vite hashes asset filenames at build time, so a hand-written precache list would be fragile. A better approach is to use a runtime caching strategy where the service worker caches requests as they happen rather than trying to precache specific files.

For the manifest and service worker registration, I'll use relative paths and reference `import.meta.env.BASE_URL` to handle GitHub Pages subpaths correctly. I'll create a branded SVG icon—a rounded square with a white lightning bolt—and reference it in the manifest for both regular and maskable purposes, plus as the apple-touch-icon and favicon.

The rest of the app—the brand store, admin page, adaptive frame, tour, count-up, and install prompt—is all pure application code with no dependencies or build step needed. I won't be able to run typecheck or build verification in the browser, but the dev tools are still available.

Actually, the Vite dev server should still be running from earlier since the terminal process runs asynchronously and disabling the tool doesn't kill it. The browser page is still active at localhost:5173, so my file edits will hot-reload automatically. I can verify visually with screenshots and use `get_errors` to check for type and compile errors instead of running `tsc` directly.

So my revised approach is to implement everything without new dependencies or terminal commands: hand-roll the PWA with icon, manifest, service worker, and registration logic; build out the brand system with types and defaults; then layer in the admin features and UI components.

For the brand system, I'm setting up a separate Zustand store for managing active brands and custom brand definitions, keeping it independent from the demo store so resets don't affect saved brands. The admin page will let users switch brands, add custom ones with name and accent color, upload logos as data URLs, and export/import the brand configuration. I'll also add an adaptive device frame that goes full-bleed on mobile but shows a frame on desktop, plus a guided tour system with anchors on key elements and auto-advance capabilities that can navigate routes and trigger store actions. The final polish will include count-up animations for KPIs, an install button, and route transitions—keeping it tasteful without going overboard.

This is a substantial scope, so I need to be methodical and keep the build green throughout.

For the tour system, I'm thinking through how to make it reliable and visually clear. The tour will set the persona, navigate to the right route, highlight the target element with a bounding box and caption, and optionally run a store action to show live changes. Since the TourOverlay lives inside the Router, it can use `useNavigate` directly. I'll keep tour state in its own small Zustand store with just the active step index and playing status, then have the overlay component drive navigation and persona changes via effects. The tour steps themselves will be data-driven with optional hooks for actions and auto-advance delays.

Defining tour steps as structured data...

When a step changes, the overlay will set the persona if specified, navigate to the route, run any action, then locate and highlight the target element. For completing tasks during the tour, I can call `completeTask` with a known seed task ID, which is idempotent if the user already completed it. If a target element isn't found, the caption will just center instead of breaking. I'm planning to add `data-tour` anchors throughout the UI—on the persona switcher, copilot button, priority cards, task evidence, control tower health metrics, and other key sections—so the tour can highlight them reliably by route and container-level target rather than deep nested elements.

Writing the service worker with runtime caching... I'll also store the index under multiple URL variations so navigation fallback works reliably. For the theme color, I'll update the meta tag whenever the brand accent changes. I'm setting up a small PWA module with a Zustand store to capture the install prompt event and expose it so an Install button can trigger it, plus handling iOS separately with a hint to use Share → Add to Home Screen since iOS doesn't support beforeinstallprompt. The manifest will use a neutral-leaning name like "wattsRus Store Ops" with short_name "wattsRus", though users can update it if they rebrand permanently. I'm creating a maskable SVG icon with a rounded rectangle and bolt design that'll work across different device contexts.

Writing SVG code... I'm going with the Material Design lightning bolt for a cleaner filled appearance, scaling it to fit centered in the icon with proper padding for maskable icons. Now I'm setting up the PWA files: creating the manifest with the accent color and background, the service worker, and updating the HTML head with the necessary meta tags and icon references. I'll also add helper functions to handle service worker registration and install prompts, then move on to building out the brand system and admin interface. I'll keep the existing favicon link and add the manifest plus touch icons, updating the favicon href to point to the SVG for consistency. For the service worker registration in main.tsx, I'm guarding it behind a production check to avoid caching issues during development while still enabling the PWA in the built version. The install prompt listener can stay unguarded since beforeinstallprompt only fires on production HTTPS anyway. Now I'm setting up a Zustand store for PWA state management...

Writing PWA store...

Then I'm capturing the beforeinstallprompt and appinstalled events to update the store, and starting on the brand system with an interface for brand objects and a set of built-in brands with their accent colors.

For the TopBar, I'm deciding to keep the brand name simple and just color the icon mark with the accent color rather than splitting the text styling. I'm setting up a separate persisted store for brand management that tracks the active brand, custom brands, and provides methods to add, update, and delete brands, with a selector that combines built-in and custom brands.

Now I'm creating a hook that applies the active brand by updating CSS variables across the entire app—setting primary colors, ring colors, sidebar variants, and accent colors to match the brand's accent color, including a translucent version for the accent background to maintain visual cohesion.

I'll also update the page's theme-color meta tag and document title to reflect the active brand name. The TopBar will display the brand's mark (either a logo image or a Zap icon) alongside the brand name, styled with the primary color classes. For the admin page, I'm building a full brand management interface with a grid of brand cards showing the name, accent swatch, and logo preview, plus controls to switch brands, edit them, delete custom ones, and add new brands with a form for name, accent color, and logo upload. There'll also be buttons to reset to the default brand, export custom brands as JSON, and import brands from a file, along with a toggle for full-screen store view and an install app button.

The admin route will be accessible via a gear icon in the user dropdown menu and directly at `/admin`. For the DeviceFrame, I'm adding a `useIsStandalone()` hook to detect PWA standalone mode, then in the StoreLayout I'll decide whether to render the full-bleed layout or keep the tablet bezel based on standalone mode, screen size, and a force-native toggle. The DeviceFrame will accept a `framed` prop to conditionally render the bezel and status bar, keeping the header and tabs markup shared between both layouts. On mobile or in standalone mode, the layout goes full width with a simpler header and sticky tabs.

For the TopBar in standalone PWA mode, I'm keeping it responsive rather than hiding it entirely—it's needed for the demo to switch personas, and the colleague view is still the downloadable version. The content area will use flexible height with a minimum based on viewport minus the topbar, and I'll default the landing view to the Store when launched as a standalone PWA for a more native app feel.

Now for the guided tour system: I'm building a tour store with state for active/playing status, current step index, and controls like start/stop/next/prev. The TourOverlay component reads this state and applies each step—setting the persona, navigating, or triggering actions—then locates the target element with retries and renders a spotlight effect. The spotlight uses a fixed overlay with a highlight box positioned at the target's rect, creating a darkened backdrop with a hole via box-shadow, and displays a caption card with the step title, counter, and navigation controls. If the target isn't found, the caption centers as a modal. Auto-advance happens when playing, and reaching the last step stops the tour. I need to add data-tour anchors throughout the interface—TopBar elements like brand, persona switcher, copilot button, sources, and the DailyBrief priorities section.

The tour itself walks through eleven steps: starting with a centered welcome explaining the copilot, then moving through estate health and pillar themes on the HQ view, live signals, the copilot features, switching to Store persona to show prioritized actions and completing a task to demonstrate impact, then Regional view for exceptions, Impact page for ROI, and finishing with a reset prompt. Each step auto-advances after about 5-6 seconds with manual controls available throughout.

For polish, I'm adding a count-up animation hook that transitions numeric values smoothly over 600ms using requestAnimationFrame, which I'll apply to key spots like the estate health ring and impact KPIs. I'm also adding route fade transitions by wrapping the outlet content with a fade-in animation keyed to the pathname. I'll skip confetti on task completion to avoid extra dependencies, and I'll add an "Install app" button in the admin section rather than cluttering the main interface.

I can't run a production build to fully verify the PWA installation, so I'll make sure the code is correct and note that the user can run `npm run build && npm run preview` to test it themselves. I'm going to do targeted edits to add `data-tour` attributes rather than modifying every file, then start writing the PWA static files and updating index.html. I'm creating an SVG icon with a bolt symbol that fits cleanly in a 24x24 viewBox.

data.content:
Heads-up: I've been switched back to **Ask mode** (read-only) — my file-creation and terminal tools are disabled, so I can't apply these changes right now. But all of this is very doable. Here's a straight answer to each question plus the implementation plan, then you can flip to **Agent mode** and say "go" and I'll build it.

## 1. Is it a PWA yet? No — here's what's needed
Right now it's a plain SPA: no manifest, no service worker, not installable. The good news is the foundation is already PWA-friendly (relative `base: './'` in [vite.config.ts](vite.config.ts) + HashRouter, so it works on GitHub Pages subpaths). To make it installable I'll add:

- **Web app manifest** (`name`, `short_name`, `theme_color`, `display: standalone`, `start_url`, `orientation`).
- **Service worker** for offline caching (so it opens with no network after first load — essential since you're demoing offline).
- **Icons**: a branded SVG/PNG set (192, 512, maskable) + `apple-touch-icon` for iOS.
- **iOS meta tags** + an **install button** (`beforeinstallprompt` on Android/Chrome; "Share → Add to Home Screen" hint on iOS).

I'd use `vite-plugin-pwa` (Workbox) — cleanest, auto-generates the SW and handles updates.

> One caveat to set expectations: the **installed home-screen icon and name are fixed at install time** from the manifest. Your runtime rebrand (next section) changes the *in-app* logo/name/colour, but not the icon already on someone's home screen. Default manifest = wattsRus.

## 2. Do you need the tablet outline on phone? No
For an installed phone app, the bezel looks wrong. I'll make [DeviceFrame.tsx](src/components/layout/DeviceFrame.tsx) **adaptive**:
- **Installed PWA / narrow phone** → full-bleed, native-feel (no bezel).
- **Desktop (presenting)** → keep the tablet frame so it still reads as "in-store device."
- A toggle in `/admin` to force full-screen, and default to the **Store view** when launched as an installed PWA (since that's the colleague experience you're handing out).

Detection: `window.matchMedia('(display-mode: standalone)')` + viewport width.

## 3. Auto-walkthrough — yes
Two flavours; I recommend the second:
- **Coached tour** — spotlight tooltips you click through (Next/Back).
- **Auto-play "demo mode"** — hands-free: switches persona, navigates routes, highlights elements, and *performs key actions* (e.g. completes the £3,200 task so KPIs visibly move), with **Play/Pause/Next/Prev/Exit + progress**.

I'll hand-roll a small engine (no dependency) driven by a step list and `data-tour` anchors I add to key elements:

```ts
interface TourStep {
  persona?: 'HQ' | 'Regional' | 'Store'
  route?: string
  target?: string      // data-tour anchor to spotlight
  title: string; body: string
  action?: () => void  // e.g. completeTask('task-sig-214-promo')
  advanceMs?: number   // auto-advance when playing
}
```
A scripted ~90-second run: HQ control tower → signals explorer → store priorities → complete-with-evidence → Copilot clienteling → region SLAs → impact. Launchable from the top bar (and auto-offer on first visit).

## 4. `/admin` brand manager — yes, with a GitHub caveat
I'll add an admin view to change **name, logo, and accent colour on the fly**, applied at runtime by rewriting CSS variables (`--primary` etc.), the `theme-color`, the document title, and the top-bar logo. Stored in a **separate persisted store** so "Reset demo" never wipes your brands.

```ts
interface Brand { id: string; name: string; accent: string; logo?: string /* dataURL */; builtIn?: boolean }
```
Features: switch active brand, **add/upload logo** (file → base64 dataURL), edit, **delete** (custom ones), reset to wattsRus default, and **export/import JSON**. Default = wattsRus + current icon, with a couple of example presets so switching is instantly visible.

**The "stored on GitHub" reality** (important): a pure client-side PWA *cannot write back to your repo at runtime* without a backend — the only way would be embedding a GitHub token in the app, which is insecure (anyone could extract it). So the practical model is:
- **Built-in presets live in the repo** (committed = literally on GitHub) in a `brands.ts` file.
- **Runtime uploads save on the device** (localStorage) — perfect for "upload one on the move during a demo."
- **Export → commit**: export your custom brand as JSON (logo embedded) and drop it into the repo to make it a permanent default.

If you genuinely want uploads to persist *to GitHub from the browser*, that needs a tiny serverless function (e.g. a GitHub Action/Netlify/Azure Function) holding the token — I can outline that, but it's beyond a static PWA. For your use case (quick rebrand per demo), localStorage + export is the right fit.

## 5. More interactivity & polish
High-value additions I'd make:
- **Count-up KPI numbers** + animated route fade transitions (tasteful).
- **Command palette** (Ctrl/⌘-K) to jump to any store/page/persona — great on stage.
- **Clienteling basket**: add the recommended item + accessories + care plan with a running total (makes the Copilot feel real).
- **Confetti / "£ protected" burst** when a task completes; **SLA breach toasts** as the clock ticks.
- **Install button + offline-ready banner**; **deep-link share**; **print/export a one-page store report**.
- **Dark mode** (tokens already support it); accessibility/focus pass.

## My recommended scope (say "go with your recommendations")
PWA via `vite-plugin-pwa` · adaptive frame (full-bleed on phone) · auto-play guided tour · `/admin` brand manager (localStorage + export/import, wattsRus default) · count-up + route transitions + command palette + clienteling basket + install button.

Good questions — these are the right things to nail down before building, because they change the architecture. Here's my thinking on each, then the updated plan.

## Your three questions, answered

### 1. Both coached tour *and* demo auto-play? — Yes, and it's cheap
They serve genuinely different moments, so having both is worth it:

| Mode | Best for | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Coached tour** | Someone who downloaded the PWA and is exploring solo; you narrating at your own pace | Spotlight + tooltip, manual **Next/Back**, actions fire as you advance |
| **Demo auto-play** | You on stage, hands-free, talking over it | Auto-advances on timers, performs actions automatically, **Play/Pause** |

The key design point: **both share one tour engine and one step list** — a single `mode: 'coached' | 'autoplay'` flag just changes whether steps auto-advance. So "both" costs almost nothing extra.

On *where* the toggle lives, I'd split it:
- **Admin sets policy** (persists across demos): which mode **auto-launches on first open** (perfect for the PWA hand-out), default persona, and a "reset onboarding" button.
- **The app surface provides the launch buttons** (top bar): both modes one tap away during a live session.

Burying the actual Play button in `/admin` would slow you down on stage — admin is for setup, the top bar is for running it.

### 2. Whole app on phones/desktops, or store-only? — Show the whole app
Given the new intent ("showcase it installing as a real app on various devices"), **locking installed users to the store view undersells it.** The "wow" is the *entire platform* — HQ control tower, region cockpit, store — working offline on any device. So:
- **Persona switching available everywhere**, fully responsive.
- The store view just *happens* to be the most phone-shaped; no need to restrict to it.
- HQ tables/charts already reflow; I'll do a responsive pass so they're comfortable on a phone.

### 3. Do you need to detect "presentation mode"? — No, and you shouldn't (for layout)
This is the one where I'd change my earlier advice. Auto-detecting device/standalone to swap layouts is **fragile on stage** (the classic live-demo failure), and worse, the **fake tablet bezel actively undermines your message** — if you're showing "this installs as a *real* desktop app," wrapping it in a mock tablet frame makes it look like a prototype.

So:
- **Drop auto-detection for layout.** Make the app full-bleed and native-feeling **everywhere by default.**
- Turn the tablet bezel into an **optional admin toggle** ("Show in-store device frame," **off by default**) — there only for when you specifically want to tell the "colleague on the shop floor" story.
- Keep **one tiny bit** of standalone detection, used for just one thing: **hide the "Install" button once the app is already installed.** Not for layout.

Net: you control exactly what shows, nothing auto-misfires, and the default reads as a real installable app.

---

## Updated plan (v4 — PWA + interactivity)

**A. PWA & install**
- Manifest (`standalone`, `theme_color`, `start_url`), service worker (offline-first), branded icons (192/512/maskable) + `apple-touch-icon` + iOS meta.
- **Install button** (Android/Chrome `beforeinstallprompt`; iOS "Add to Home Screen" hint), auto-hidden once installed.
- Caveat unchanged: the **home-screen icon/name are fixed at install time** from the manifest (default wattsRus); runtime rebrand changes the *in-app* identity, not an already-installed icon.

**B. Layout**
- Remove forced tablet frame; **full-bleed, responsive everywhere**; persona switch on all devices.

- Responsive pass on HQ/region tables and charts for phones.

**C. Guided experiences (one engine, two modes)**
- Tour engine + `data-tour` anchors on key elements; spotlight overlay; Play/Pause/Next/Back/Exit + progress.
- Scripted ~90s run: HQ control tower → signals explorer → store priorities → **complete task with evidence (KPIs move)** → Copilot clienteling → region SLAs → impact.
- Both **coached** and **autoplay** modes from the top bar.

**D. `/admin` console** (persisted separately from demo data, so Reset never wipes it)
- **Branding:** active brand · name · accent colour · logo upload · presets · add/edit/delete · **export/import JSON** · reset to wattsRus default.
- **Demo & onboarding:** auto-launch on first open (off / coached / autoplay) · default persona · reset "seen tour."
- **Appearance:** in-store device frame toggle (off) · dark mode.
- GitHub reality unchanged: built-in presets live in the repo (= on GitHub); runtime uploads save on-device; **export → commit** to make one permanent.

**E. Interactivity polish**
